description: A fine and hard, well-fired ware with smooth fracture, and light gray (Munsell 5YR, 7II) surfaces and gray (Munsell 5YR, 5/1) core. Inclusions are gray, rounded (up to 1mm; 2%) and void, rounded to sub-rectangular (lmm-2mm; 10/0) traces of possible vegetable/chaff prints. Walls thickness ranges between 3mm and Icm. This ware is usually plain with very few exceptions of decorated incised sherds and projected bands and traces of wheel making/turning. This type of ware is mainly used for funerary purposes but also found in settlements.
origin: South and south-eastern Iran; east and south-eastern Arabia
parallels: Tombs at Hili: Tomb 1059 (PI.70, figs. X, Y; P1.79; PI.80); Tomb B (PI.72, figs. D, H, P, 0; P1.74, figs. F, M; P1.81, figs. A-L; P1.82; P1.83; P1.84; P1.90, figs. D, E, K, L, N-P; P1.91, figs. A-J, M, N) & Tomb-H (P1.72, fig. G) as well as from the settlement at Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: 153-158: Pl.II0, fig. A; P1.122, figs. A-D); Frifelt 1991: Figs. 83-85: 49 and (Frifelt 1995: fig. 235, pp: 173; also pp: 81-88, 100-108); Tomb A at Hili North (Cleuziou & Vogt 1985: figs. 7: 263, 8: 268).

description: Black-on-Gray fine painted ware with smooth fracture and hard firing. The surface is light reddish gray (Munsell lOR, 5/1) and the core is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 5/6) and/or dark reddish gray (Munsell 2.5YR, 5/1). Inclusions are white to gray, rounded of less than 1mm size and covers I % of the sherd. Walls thickness is c 2-6 mm. This ware is wheel-made.
parallels: Hili 8 settlement (Cleuziou 1978/9: fig.30, No.6, pp: 58); Tombs at Hili (al- Tikriti 1981: 153-156): Tomb 1059 (PI. 78, figs. A-B, E; PI. 79, figs. B, G, 1, N; P1.80, figs. A-C, E-H); Tomb B (P1.81, figs. A-F-H, J-L; P1.82, figs, A-H, l-Q; P1.83, figs. A- G, J-K, P-X; PI.84, figs. C, E, G, H, K; Pls.155-156) & Tomb-H (PI. 72, fig. G) The settlement and tombs at Umm an-Nar Island: (al-Tikriti 1981: 165 & 175-180: P1.135, figs. C); Frifelt 1991: Figs. 76-82: 46-48; 120-121: 62; 142: 71; 176-178: 82 and (Frifelt 1995: figs. 92-93, pp: 64; also pp: 81-88, 100-108); Frifelt 1979b: figs. 4-5: 46; Tomb A, Hili North (Vogt 1985b: Pis. 25-26); Tomb B at Ajman (al-Tikriti 1989b: PIs. 43, 55A); al-Sufouh (Benton 1996: figs. 122-124); Tomb A at Hili North (Cleuziou & Vogt 1985: figs. 7: 263, 8: 268)

class_name: Umm an-Nar Fine Red Painted Ware
description: Fine, wheel-made and well-fired red ware with conchoidal fracture. The surface is light red (Munsell lOR, 6/8) to red (Munsell lOR, 4/8) and the core is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6). The ware includes gray to brown rounded inclusions of less than 1mm size and covers 1% of the sherd. The whole exterior body is coated with a red slip and bands with a black painting; however, some sherds are plain and undecorated. The inner surface is reddish yellow, often with black painted bands around and under the rim and sometimes red wash on and/or inside the rim. Walls thickness is c.I-8 mm. This ware is wheel made.
parallels: Hili 8 settlement (Cleuziou 1978/9: fig.22-23, pp: 54-55); Tombs at Hili (al- Tikriti 1981: 151-153): Tomb B (PI.69, figs, J, Q; PI. 71: PI.72, figs. B, C, E. F; Pl.74, figs. B, C; PI.90, figs. A-C, E, F, G-N, Q-S; PI.91, figs. A-J, M, N, 0, P) & Tomb-H (PI.72, fig. J); Ghanadha 1(al-Tikriti 1985: Plate 7: A-F); The settlement and tombs at Umm an-Nar Island in al-Tikriti 1981: 164 & 172-175: (PI.106, figs. B-E; Pl.I07, figs. A-C, E, F; PI.128, figs. B, F-H; PI.129, fig. G; PI.130, figs. A-E, G, H; P1.131, figs. D-L; PI.132, figs, A-F; P1.133, figs. A-D; PI. 134, figs. E, G); Frifelt 1991: Figs. 68-75: 44-46; 104: 59; 110: 60; 112: 60; 115-116: 61; 145: 72; 154-156: 75; 159: 75; 162: 76; 171: 79; 175: 81; and (Frifelt 1995: fig. 91, pp: 64; figs. 226-230, pp: 169-172; also pp: 81-88, 100-108); Tomb A, Hili North (Vogt 1985b: Pis. 25-26); Tomb B at Ajman (al-Tikriti 1989b: Pis. 39, 40, 42, 50-53); al-Sufouh (Benton 1996: figs. 57-107)

class_name: Umm an-Nar Fine Black Painted Ware
description: Fine, well-fired ware with conchoidal fracture. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6) and the core is light gray (Munsell 5YR, 7 II) and reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6). The ware includes gray to brown rounded inclusions of less than 1mm size and covers 2% of the sherd. Walls thickness is c.1 mm-l.3cm. This ware is similar to UMFRPW but with light gray to reddish yellow core and without the red coating but sometimes with black coating. Decoration includes black painted bands mainly on, around and below the rim and sometimes the body, however, some sherds are undecorated.
parallels: Tombs at Hili (aI-Tikriti 1981: 151-153): Tomb B (PI.69, figs, J, Q;_PI. 71: P1.72, figs. B, C, E, F; P1.74, figs. B, C; P1.90, figs. A-C, E, F, G-N, Q-S; P1.91, figs. A- J, M, N, 0, P) & Tomb-H (PI.72, fig. J). The settlement and tombs at Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: 164 & 172-175): (PI.I06, figs. B-E; PI.I07, figs. A-C, E, F; P1.128. figs. B, F-H; P1.129, fig. G; P1.130, figs. A-E, G, H; P1.131, figs. D-L; P1.132, figs, A-F: P1.133, figs. A-D; PI. 134, figs. E, G) and Frifelt 1991: Figs. 59-67: 41-44; 93-103: 52- 58: 105-106: 59;108-109: 60; Ill: 60; 117-118: 61; 136: 67; 138-141: 69-70; 143-144: 71;146-153: 72-74;157: 74;161: 76;163-166: 77;172-174: 79-80; Frifelt 1979b: figs. 2- 3: 45, 9: 48, 18: 56; Frifelt 1995: fig. 96, pp: 66-67; Fig. 238, pp: 175; also pp: 81-88, 100-108); Tomb A, Hili North (Vogt 1985b: PIs. 25-26); Tomb B at Bidya (al-Tikriti 1989a: PIs. 39. 40, 42, 50-53); al-Sufouh (Benton 1996: figs. 57-107); Tomb A at Hili North (Cleuziou & Vogt 1985: figs. 6: 261,10: 274)

description: One fine, well-fired sherd of glassy fabric? with smooth fracture. The sherd is part of the vessel rim. The surface is reddish gray (Munsell lOR, 611) and the core is bluish gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 6/1). Decoration includes nail/bone prints or incised hatching decoration under the rim. Walls are very thin, varies between 1mm and 3mm. The ware is wheel-made.
origin: South and south-eastern Iran; east and south-eastern Arabia?
parallels: Tombs at Hili & the settlement and tombs at Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: 156-158): Tomb-1059 (PI.86, figs. A-C, L; P1.87, figs. A, C), Tomb-B (PI.81, fig. M; PI. 88; P1.89), the settlement at Umm an-Nar Island (PI. 11 0, figs. B-F; PI.135, figs. E- F); Frifelt 1991: Figs. 122-124: 62-63 and (Frifelt 1995: fig.68, pp: 54; also pp: 81-88, 100-108);

class_name: Umm an-Nar Thin Fine Red Ware
description: Very fine, well fired, red ware with conchoidal fracture. The surface is light red (Munsell lOR, 6/8) to red (Munsell lOR, 5/8) and the core is red (Munsell lOR, 5/8) to weak red (Munsell lOR, 4/4). The surface of this ware is soft with stony touch and its walls thickness is c.I-6 mm.
parallels: This ware is similar to the UMFRPW but the core is darker and no traces at paint were noted as well as the surface appears harder (Frifelt 1991: Figs. 107: 60; 113- 114: 60; 158: 75; Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108); Tomb A, Hili North (Vogt 1985b: Pis. 24).

class_name: Umm an-Nar Heavy Fine Red Ware
description: Heavy, hard-fired, fine red ware with conchoidal fracture. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/6) to dark red (Munsell 2.5YR, 4/6), and the core is greenish gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 6/1) to dark gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 4/). The ware includes gray rounded inclusions of 1-1.4 mm size and covers 1% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 1 cm
parallels: The settlement of Umm an-Nar Island: Frifelt 1991: Figs. 107: 60; 113-114: 60; 158: 75; Frifelt 1995: fig. 231, pp: 172 & figs. 233-234, pp: 173; also pp: 81-88, 100- 108.

description: Thin, fine hard-fired ware with conchoidal fracture. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/6) with a gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 6/) sandwich and gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 6/) to light red (Munsell lOR, 6/8) core. The ware includes gra) to brown rounded inclusions of up to 1mm size and covers 3% of the sherd. Walls thickness is c.1-6 mm. This ware is similar to the UMHFRW but the walls are thinner and the core is darker as well as the inclusions are denser.
parallels: The settlement ofUmm an-Nar Island: Frifelt 1991: Figs. 107: 60; 113-114: 60; 158: 75; Frifelt 1995: fig. 231, pp: 172 & figs. 233-234, pp: 173; also pp: 81-88,100- 108.

description: One sherd with very polished and shiny surfaces, and hard like stone with fine, conchoidal fracture, and hard firing. The sherd is part of vessel with possible rounded rim? The colour of the surface is dark red (Munsell 2.5YR, 3/6) and red (Munsell 2.5YR, 5/6) and the core is dusky red (2.5YR, 3/4) and dark reddish gray (Munsell 2.5YR, 411). Inclusions include gray rounded (less than 1 mm; 10/0) and shiny mica on surfaces and core. The surface includes scratching of possible tool/wheel making traces. Walls thickness is c.2 mm-l cm.

Coarse ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6), and the core is bluish gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA~ 511). The ware includes gray rounded and rectangular (1-3 mm; 6%) and void? traces of chaff (rectangular) (1-3 mm; 30/0) inclusions. This was is light with walls' thickness of c.1--4 mm. Similar to the UMGCC but with darker core.
parallels: The settlement at the Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: 163-164; Pl.120, figs. A-B; Pl.121, figs. A-D; Pl.122, figs. A-E) & (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108); and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Fig. 131: 65)

class_name: Umm an-Nar Gray Core Coarse Ware
description: Thin, coarse ware with smooth fracture, and medium firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6) and the core is light gray (Munsell 5YR, 711) to red (Munsell lOR, 5/8). The ware includes white, gray & brown rounded inclusions of up to 1mm size and covers 50/0 of the sherd. Walls thickness is c.1-8 mm. This ware is abundant in the settlement sites and rare in the tombs and thus, it is called the domestic coarse ware.
parallels: The settlement at the Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: 163-164; PI.120, figs. A-B; P1.121, figs. A-D; P1.122, figs. A-E) & (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108): and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Fig. 131: 65); Tomb A, Hili North (Vogt 1985b: PI. 23)

description: Medium coarse gray ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is gray (Munsell 5YR, 611) to light greenish gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 7II) and the core is light gray (Munsell 7.5YR, 7/1) to reddish brown (Munsell 5YR, 5/3). The outer surface is gray to light greenish gray while the inner surface is gray to reddish brown. The ware includes gray and white rounded to angular inclusions of 1-3mm size and covers 2% of the sherd with some traces of wheel making/turning. Several sherds are decorated with incised horizontal and wavy lines. Walls thickness is c.3-8 mm. This ware is similar to UMTCW but with thinner walls and incised decoration but unpainted.
parallels: Sherds 1424, 2993 & 4869 have similar parallels from Hili, tomb-1059 (al- Tikriti 1981: PI. 86, figs. M, Q, R) & the settlement at Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: PI. 11 0, figs. C-F) & (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108), and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Figs. 132: 65; 134: 66; Frifelt 1979b: figs. 6-7: 47); al-Sufouh (Benton 1996: fig. 126); Tomb A at Hili North (Cleuziou & Vogt 1985: figs. 9: 270)

description: Thick, well fired reddish yellow coarse ware with conchoidal fracture. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 7.5YR, 7/6), and the core is reddish yellow (Munsell 7.5YR, 7/6) to gray (Munsell 5YR, 6/1). The ware includes gray and brown rounded (1-2 mm; 40/0) and void? traces of chaff prints (rectangular) (1-3 mm; 1%) inclusions. Most of the sherds belong to large and thick jars. Walls' thickness is c.3-8 mm. This type is similar to the UMTCW but without coating and its fabric is less coarse or semi-coarse, and its core is darker in colour.
parallels: The settlement at Umm an-Nar Island (al-Tikriti 1981: 18L P1.134) & (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108), and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Fig. 88: 51)

description: Medium coarse hard-fired ware with thick walls, and conchoidal fracture. The surface is light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/8) and the core is light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/8). The ware includes gray to brown rounded inclusions of up to 1.5mm size and covers 7% of the sherd. Decoration includes red coating with wheel making/turning traces on the inner surface. The ware is heavy with walls' thickness of c.4 mm-l.4 cm. It is wheel- made.
parallels: Hili (al-Tikriti 1981: 158): Tomb-1059 (PI.96; P1.97, figs. A-B: P1.98; P1.99; PI. I 00; and the settlement at the Umm an-Nar Island (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108), and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Fig. 135: 67)

description: Medium coarse red ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6), and the core is light greenish gray (Munsell I CHROMA, 7II) to red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8). The ware includes gray and white rounded inclusions of 1-2 mm size and covers 40/0 of the sherd. Some sherds might have traces of painting or coating but badly eroded and the inner surface of some sherds have light gray colour. Walls thickness is c.1 mm-l cm. This ware is similar to the UMTCW but with thinner walls.
parallels: Hili (al-Tikriti 1981: 158): Tomb-1059 (P1.96; P1.97, figs. A-B; P1.98; P1.99; Pl.I00; and the settlement at Umm an-Nar Island (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108) and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Fig. 135: 67)

class_name: Umm an-Nar Very Pale Brown Ware
description: Thin, very pale brown coarse ware with red colour on the inner surface, smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is very pale brown (Munsell 10YR, 7/4). and the core is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 6/6). The ware includes gray rounded inclusions of 1 mm size and covers 4% of the sherd. Walls thickness is 1-5 mm. This ware is similar to the UMCW but the core is lighter and coating traces were possibly in the inner side of the walls?
parallels: Hili (al-Tikriti 1981: 158): Tomb-1059 (PI.96; P1.97. figs. A-B: P1.98: P1.99; PI. 100; and the settlement at Umm an-Nar Island (Frifelt 1995: 81-88, 100-108). and the graves (Frifelt 1991: Figs. 86-88: 50-51: 90-92: 52: 125-130: 63-65: 137: 68)

description: These are small badly eroded, fragmentary sherds of coarse and rough fracture, and soft firing. The surface's colour is unclear and the core tends to be gray?

Pink, coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is pink (Munsell 7.SY, 7/4), and the core is dark gray (Munsell 7.SY, 411) core with gray and brown rounded and sub-rectangular inclusions of up to 2 mm size and covers 30/0 of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1-S mm
total_sherds: 9 sherds, and one possible sherd from site CS.1.S.4 (P02093)

description: Medium coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface and the core are reddish yellow (Munsell SYR, 6/6) with gray and brown rounded and angular inclusions of up to 2 mm size and covers 30/0 of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 1 cm
parallels: Two sherds 804 and 1428 have possible unpainted footed-bases with parallels from Shimal Settlement Fabrics 1-3 (Franke-Vogt & Velde 1987: figs.43, Nos.I-10: Velde 1992: 62-64) Sharm (Barker 2002: Fig.4, Nos. 2-16, pp: 7; Fig.5, SP- L SP-6, SP- 466); Tell Abraq (Potts 1991: 41, figs.39, nos. 10, 12); also Velde 1992, 2003: Potts 1990c: 56-95; 1991: 36-75

description: Thin, light, medium coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is gray (Munsell 5YR, 5/1) to light gray (Munsell 5YR, 7/1), and the core is gray (Munsell 5YR, 5/1) with gray and brown rounded inclusions of up to 2 mm size and covers 2% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.I-5 mm. Some sherds are badly eroded.
total_sherds: 4 sherds with possible 5 sherds from site CS.2.56.3 (P02383-P02387)

description: Red, medium firing coarse ware with black painted decoration and smooth fracture. The surface and core are light red (Munsell lOR, 6/8) with gray and brown rounded inclusions of up to 1.5 mm size and covers 20/0 of the sherd. Walls thickness is c 1-6 mm. This ware is thin and light. Some sherds are badly eroded
parallels: Two sherds (4881, 4882) of simple flat or straight rims of possible beakers have possible parallels from Bidya 1 (al-Tikriti 1989a: PI. 70, A-B); also Velde 2003: figs. 1.5- 10, pp: 103); two sherds (402,2458) with traces of black or red paint of possible painted beakers that have possible examples from Bidya 1 (al-Tikriti 1989a: PIs. 94, A: 63, A-C: 64, A-B) and Hili 8 (Cleuziou 1989a: P1.31, Nos.2-5); Shimal (Velde 1992: 34); also Velde 1992,2003; Potts 1990c: 56-95; 1991: 36-75

description: Hard-fired coarse ware with smooth fracture. The surface and core are light red (Munsell lOR, 6/8) to light gray (Munsell 5YR, 7/1) with gray and brown rounded inclusions of up to 1.5 mm size and covers 40/0 of the sherd. Walls thickness is cl-8 mm. This class was difficult to sign to either Wadi Suq or Early Iron Age, thus it was assigned to the broad chronological period.

Black-on-red, coarse thin and well fired ware with smooth fracture. The surface is black-on-red and the core is red core (Munsell 2.5 YR, 5/6) with black to brown , rounded inclusions of 1 mm size & covers c.30/0 of the sherd. The outer surface is black (painted/coated?) on red. The inner surface also has traces of black painting/coating? Walls are thin with a thickness of c.4-6 mm. OriJ:in: Local?
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: pis, 49, 50-52)

description: Light fine ware and thin walls as well as well fired fabric and smooth fracture. The surface is red (Munsell lOR, 5/6) and the core is dark red (Munsell 2.5 YR, 4/8) with gray rounded inclusions of 1 mm size and covers c.1 % of the sherd. Walls thickness is 3-6 mm
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.50, No.2; p1.47, No.1; p1.52, No.3; p1.59, No.8); Tell Abraq excavation: (Potts 1991: p1.112, nos.9,l1,14, pp: 85); AI-Madam area excavation: (Benoist, Cordoba & Mouton 1997: fig.6, nos. 10, pp: 69)

class_name: Thick Hard Fired Early Iron Age
description: Fine, thick, heavy ware with conchoidal fracture and hard firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 6/6) and the core is light greenish gray core (Munsell 1 CHROMA) with gray to brown, Oval to rounded inclusions of 1-2 mm size and covers c.2O/o of the sherd. Walls thickness is 4 mm to l.2 cm
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.51, No.6; p1.53, Nos.2, 5 & p1.56, No.7); Tell Abraq excavation: (Potts 1991: fig.112. No.5: pp: 85) & (Magee 1996: fig.2, pp: 243) & (Benoist, Cordoba & Mouton 1997: fig.7. nos. 1. 6, pp: 71 )

class_name: Thick Black Painted Early Iron Age
description: Thick, heavy well-fired ware with black paint on both surfaces, and conchoidal fracture. The surface is light red (Munsell lOR, 7/8) and the core is dark reddish gray (Munsell 2.5YR, 4/1) with gray, rounded inclusions of 1-2 mm size and covers c.3% of the sherd. Walls thickness is c.5 mm to 2.5 cm, with black paint on the exterior and interior surfaces.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.49: 1-28: p1.71: 8a-b; p1.50: 1-3)

description: Red, fine well fired ware with conchoidal fracture and red surface (Munsell lOY. 5/6) as well as gray core (Munsell 5YR, 611) with light brown. rounded inclusions of less than I mm size and covers c.1 % of the sherd. Walls thickness is c.3-8 mm Oril:in: Local?
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: pIs. 49.50)

description: Fine, thin, light hard-fired ware with smooth fracture and red surface (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) as well as gray sandwich (Munsell 5YR, 611) with brown, rounded inclusions of less than 1 mm size and covers c.1 % of the sherd. Decoration includes dusky red painted bands on both interior and exterior surfaces. Walls' thickness is c3-5 mm.
parallels: Hili 2 settlement excavation (AI-Rahman 1978/9: pp: 9 & fig. 6: pp: 18): Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: pI. 71, Nos.8a & 11: p1.49, Nos.12; P1.50, Nos.1-3); Tell Abraq excavation: (Potts 1991: fig. 1 12. no.6, pp: 85) Magee (1996: fig.3, pp: 242); Sharm excavation (Barker 2002): Fig.28, Nos. 11: SP-247, 12: SP-230 & 13: SP-457, pp: 34; Fig. 25: No.9: SP-252/448, pp: 30 and fig.26, pp: 32)

description: Thin, fine ware with smooth fracture and hard firing with light red surface (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/6) and red core (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8). The ware includes very small gray to brown, rounded inclusions on both surfaces (exterior and interior) of up to l.5 mm size and covers c.1 % of the sherd. Sometimes the surfaces include dark red painting. Walls' thickness is c.3-8 mm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.23: 1-13)

class_name: Very Thin Coarse Early Iron Age
description: Very thin and rough coarse ware with medium firing. The surface is light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/6) and the core is pale red (Munsell lOR, 7/4) with gray to brown, rounded and angular inclusions of up to 1 mm size and covers c.4% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.3-5 mm
parallels: Tell Abraq excavation: (Magee 1996: fig. 1, pp: 242)

description: Thick, very coarse, pink ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface is pink (Munsell 5YR, 8/4) and the core is gray (Munsell 5YR, 6/1) with gray to brown, angular and/or sub-rectangular inclusions of up to 6 mm size and covers c. 7° ° of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1 cm to 2.5 cm
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.47: II & pI.48:1)

class_name: Very Pale Brown Early Iron Age
description: Coarse hard-fired ware with smooth fracture and very pale brown surface (Munsell lOR, 7/4) and light greenish gray core (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 711) with gray to brown, rounded inclusions of up to 1 mm size and covers c.3% of the sherd.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985)

class_name: Gray Sandwich Coarse Early Iron Age
description: Thick, heavy coarse ware with rough fracture and hard firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 7.5YR, 7/6) and light gray sandwich (Munsell 7.5YR, 711) with gray to brown, rounded and angular inclusions of 1--4 mm size and covers c.6% of the sherd; Walls' thickness is c.5 mm-2 cm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985) and Tell Abraq (Potts 1990c: 96-123 & 1991); Of significance is Sherd P02163 that represents applique snake motif in relief decoration that has similar example from: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.51, No.7; p1.71, No.4); Muweilah excavation (Magee 1998a: fig.4, pp: 115) and Bithnah-44/50 (Benoist 2007: 34-54)

description: Large brown gritted coarse ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) and the core is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) or Gray (Munsell 5.5YR, 611) with large brown, square and angular inclusions/grits of 1-6 mm size and covers c.5% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is 6 mm to 1.4 cm
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: pl.47, No.6,7,15; p1.48, nos.2,3; p1.52, Nos.1-3; p1.53, nos.1.8); Tell Abraq excavation: (Potts 1991: fig. 109, pp: 82; fig. 11 0, nos.I-7, pp: 82; fig.1ll, nos. 1-4, pp: 83) & (Magee 1996: fig.2, pp: 243); AI-Madam area excavation: (Benoist, Cordoba & Mouton 1997: fig.5. no.16, pp: 65; fig.6, nos.7, 12, pp: 69); Muweilah (Magee, Pers. Comm .. August 2007).

description: Dusky red painted coarse ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface and core are red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) with gray to brown, angular inclusions on both surfaces of up to 1 mm size and covers c.30/0 of the sherd. Painted decoration of wavy and horizontal lines and bands on both surfaces, around and under the rim. neck and shoulder. Walls' thickness is c.4-6 mm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.49, Nos.3-7. 10, 15-24; p1.53, No.7; p1.58, No.14; p1.71, Nos.9-10); Tell Abraq excavation: (Magee 1996: fig.4, c: pp: 163); AI-Madam area excavation: (Benoist, Cordoba & Mouton 1997: fig.5. no.13, pp: 65; fig.6, nos.1 ,2, pp: 69)

description: Coarse, well-fired heavy ware with smooth fracture and red Light surface (Munsell lOY. 6/8) as well as red light core (Munsell lOY, 6/8) with brown, sub-rounded (up to 1.5 mm. 4%)inclusions and light-gray traces of straw? (Up to 1.5 mm. 10/0). Walls thickness is c.5mm-l cm. Some sherds include nail prints (incisions) on and around the rIm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: pI.51.nos.3-4: p1.53, Nos.11-12); Tell Abraq excavation: (Magee & Carter 1999: figA, b, d: pp: 163)

class_name: Gritted Medium Coarse Early Iron Age
description: Large gritted medium coarse ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/8) and the core is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/8), and/or gray (Munsell 5YR, 6/1) with brown to red, square and angular (l mm- up to 1 cm; 60/0) and white rounded (Up to 1 mm; 1%) inclusions and traces of straw/chaff prints. Walls' thickness is c.5 mm to 1.5 cm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: P1.65 & p1.73, Nos.3-4)

description: Thin, coarse well-fired ware, with conchoidal fracture, and reddish yellow surface (Munsell 5YR, 7/6) and red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) and sometimes reddish gra) core (Munsell lOR, 611) with gray to brown, angular and sub-rounded inclusions of 1-4 mm size and covers c.5% of the sherd. Some sherds are light greenish gray (1 CHROMA, 711) on the inner surface (6 sherds from site CS.4.3). Walls' thickness is c.2-8 mm
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.52. no.4; p1.54, Nos.5-6); Tell Abraq excavation: (Magee 1996: fig.2, pp: 243)

description: Thin, coarse ware with rough fracture and soft firing. The surface and the core are light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/6) with brown to white rounded inclusions of 1-2 mm size and covers c.2% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.2-8 mm
parallels: Tell Abraq excavation: (Magee & Carter 1999: fig.4.h, pp: 163)

description: Heavy, red-on-light-red coarse ware with conchoidal fracture and hard firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/6) and light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 617) and the core is reddish yellow (Munsell SYR, 6/8) with gray to brown rounded and oval inclusions of 1-2.5 mm size and covers c.5% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.4 mm to 1 em. Some sherds bear projected bands with incised finger/nail prints and herring-bone decoration.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.48, Nos.2,4; pl.S2.no.7; pl.SS.nos.6-8; P1.6S; p1.7I, no.6.; p1.73, Nos.3-4) ; Tell Abraq excavation: (Potts I990c: fig.II7, pp: 96; fig. 104, nos. 1-8, pp: 81; figs. lOS, 106.107: pp: 80; fig. 108, pp: 81); (Potts 1991: 103, Nos.I-3, pp: 80; fig. 104, nos.I-8, pp: 81; figs. lOS, 106, 107: pp: 80; fig. 108, pp: 81); (Magee 1996: fig.2), (Magee 1999: fig.3, pp: 44)

description: Thin, light coarse ware with rough fracture and soft firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.SYR, 6/6) and reddish black (Munsell 2.SYR, 2.S/1) and the core is light gray (Munsell lOR, 7/1) with brown to gray rounded (l mm; 2%) and white to gray sub- rectangular (l mm-I cm; 1%) inclusions. Walls' thickness is c.3 mm to 1 cm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985)

description: Thin, coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing as well as reddish yellow surfaces; both inside and outside and gray core with gray to white traces of straw/chaff prints 1-4 mm in size and covers 4% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1-4 mm.
parallels: Rumeilah Settlement excavation (Boucharlat & Lombard 1985: p1.23: 14-18)

description: Badly small eroded coarse sherds with rough fracture and soft firing. The surface is red to light red? Walls thickness is c.2 mm to up to 1 cm

Very thin and fine gray ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is gray (Munsell 10YR, 5/1), and the core is yellowish red (Munsell 5YR, 5/8) with white rounded inclusions of up to 1 mm size and covers 1% of the sherd. Walls thickness is c.I-5 mm
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001a)

description: Thin red fine ware with smooth fracture and hard firing. The surface is red (lOR, 5/8) to reddish yellow (Munsell 7.5YR, 7/6), and the core is red (Munsell lOR, 4/8) to light red (Munsell lOR, 7/8) with gray and white rounded inclusions of up to 1.5 mm size and covers 1% of the sherd. Decoration includes incised herring bones with and without hatched or pitted design? Walls' thickness is 1-6 mm.
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001a)

class_name: Large Gritted Coarse Late Iron Age
description: Thick, medium to very coarse ware with large grits and rough fracture as well as soft firing. The surface is light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/8) to red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/6), and the core is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to reddish gray (Munsell 10R, 6/1) with gray, brown and white angular, rectangular and rounded inclusions of 1-4 mm size and covers 6% of the sherd and traces of chaff/straw prints. Decoration includes incised herring bones with and without hatched or pitted design; incised wavy line(s): projected plain band(s); incised motif like sun-flower or sun with radiations? Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 2.4 cm.
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001a)

class_name: Coarse Reddish Yellow Late Iron Age
description: Thick, very coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell SYR, 7/8), and the core is gray (Munsell 2YR, 6/1) with gray and brown angular and rounded inclusions of 1-3 mm size and covers 40/0 of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.3 mm to 2 cm. Some sherds have traces of firing/burning on the exterior surface.
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001a)

description: Medium coarse gray ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface and the core are gray (Munsell 5YR, 6/1) to reddish gray (Munsell lOR, 5/1) with white and gray rounded inclusions of up to 1.5 mm size and covers 3% of the sherd. Decoration includes incised leaf decoration. Walls' thickness is c.I-6 mm
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001 a)

class_name: Coarse Late Iron Age Coarse Gray Late Iron Age
description: Thin, medium fired coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/6) to light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/6), and the core is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to dark reddish gray (Munsell 2.5YR, 511) with white, brown and gray rounded and angular inclusions of 1-3 mm size and covers 5% of the sherd. Decoration includes incised or pitted design, incised herring bones with and without hatched or pitted design? Projected circles and bands with or without incised hatched, net or cross hatched or herring bone design and/or incised wavy and horizontalline(s). Similar to the LGCLIA but finer and well fired and less gritted. Walls' thickness is 1mm to 1 cm.
parallels: This is the most common ware recovered from many areas such as Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001 a)

description: Thin, coarse light ware with smooth fracture and soft firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/6) to red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8), and the core is reddish gray (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/1) to red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) with brown and gray rounded inclusions of 1-2 mm size and covers 3% of the sherd. Decoration includes incised wavy and/or irregular lines between horizontal lines design. Some of the sherds bear signs of burning/firing on the exterior surface. Walls' thickness is c.I-8 mm
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001a)

class_name: Dark Gray Coarse Late Iron Age
description: Reddish Yellow coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 6/8), and the core is dark gray (Munsell 1 CHROMA, 4/) with gray and brown rounded and sub-rounded inclusions of up to 2 mm size and covers 20/0 of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1-8 mm.
parallels: Samad and Maysar excavations: (Yule 2001a)

description: Coarse, medium-fired ware with smooth fracture and red to yellowish surfaces and gray to light gray core. These sherds are of Iron Age but it was difficult to sign them either to Early or Late Iron Age.

Sasanian Early Islamic of possibly clinky coarse ware with sub-conchoidal fracture and hard firing. The surface is pale yellow (Munsell 2.5Y 8/4) or weak red (Munsell 2.5YR 6/3) to dark reddish grey (2.5YR 5.1) and the core is red (2.5YR 6/8- 5/6). The fabric includes white, grey and brown inclusions. The surface usually includes splashes of glaze, mainly on the interior with different colours ranges between white, green, blue and yellow. Decoration includes incised lines. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 1 cm.
parallels: Kush (Kennet 2004: 62, fig.35, pp: 129)

description: Coarse glazed ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The glaze colour varies between green, yellow and turquoise. The surface and the core are pale yellow (Munsell 2.5Y, 8/2) with gray and brown rounded inclusions of up to 1 mm size and covers 20/0 of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1-6 mm CS.4.12 (1 sherd: P.1408) + CS.2.51.1 (3 sherds: P.6111-P.6113) + CS.7.2 (1 sherd: P.7794)

Thick, heavy and very coarse ware with rough fracture and medium firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/8), and the core is reddish yellow (Munsell 5YR, 7/8) to light gray (Munsell 5YR, 7II) with large gray, brown and white angular, rounded and sub-rectangular grits inclusions of 1-3.5 mm size and covers 6% of the sherd. Some of sherds' surfaces bear gray coating? (badly preserved). Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 2 cm.
parallels: Kush: Red Speckled ware (Kennet 2004: 64; figAO, pp: 134

description: Red and gray thin coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is light red (Munsell lOR, 6/6) to reddish gray (Munsell lOR, 611), and the core is light red (Munsell lOR, 6/8) with gray and brown rounded inclusions of up to 2 mm size and covers 20/0 of the sherd. The decoration includes group of painted horizontal bands of around 2-5 mm wide and vary between pale yellow (2.5Y, 8/3) and light gray (2.5Y. 711). Walls' thickness is 1 mm to 1 cm.
parallels: Surface pottery from Julfar (de Cardi & Doe 1971: fig.13, Nos.40-42, pp: 265)

description: Thin coarse gray ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface and the core are dark gray (Munsell 7.5YR, 411) to light gray (Munsell 7.5YR, 711) with white and brown rounded inclusions of up to 1.5 mm size and covers 3% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1-8 mm. Some sherds bear incised decoration of wavy and horizontal lines.
parallels: Kush: Spotty ware (Kennet 2004: 64; fig.38, pp: 132)

description: Medium coarse, yellowish ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface and the core are pale yellow (Munsell 2.5Y, 8/3) with gray rounded inclusions of up to 2 mm size and covers 2% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to I cm.
parallels: similar to Kush: Cream pots (Kennet 2004: 64; figs.39, pp: 133) but without the incised decoration

description: Medium coarse ware with smooth fracture and hard firing. The surface is light red (Munsell lOR, 6/6 and 2.5YR, 7/6) to greenish gray (Munsell 1CHROMA, 6/1 l. and the core is light red (Munsell lOR, 7/6) to light gray (Munsell lOR, 711) with gray, white and brown rounded and angular inclusions of up to 3 mm size and covers 3% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 1.5 cm. Decoration includes some projected bands with and without wavy lines and some sherds have incised wavy and horizontal lines. usually under the rim and around the neck.
parallels: Kush: Cream pots with incised wavy decoration (Kennet 2004: 64; figs.39. pp: 133)

description: Thin plain red fine ware with smooth fracture and hard firing. The surface is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8), and the core is red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to light gray (Munsell lOR, 711) with gray rounded inclusions of up to 1.5 mm size and covers 20/0 of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c.I-8 mm
parallels: Kush: Small Grey vessels (Ken net 2004: 63; fig.37, pp: 131

description: This type of ware is well-known and described in the literature. D. Kennet (2004: 34) describes this ware as "the decorative technique of incising linear designs through a white slip before glazing. Most often, especially in the later periods, the slip is white or cream and the body is red, causing the incisions to stand out as a darker colour. The pattern created by the incised lines is sometimes filled with patches of coloured glaze-green, brown and yellow." He argues (ibid: 34) that by the early 10th century the Sgraffiato technique has started in southern Iraq, and by the 11 th-1 t h centuries it become common and well-known technique over a large area such as Iran, Afghanistan, Egypt, Syria, Northern Iraq, Central Asia and Byzantium. He also argues that at the beginning of th the 13 century the technique reached to Europe via Northern Italy (Kennet 2004: 34). This earthenware is fine to medium-coarse with smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface is usually glazed with different coloured glaze ranging between green; white, light green, olive, yellow, brown and yellowish-green. The glaze thickness varies between 1 mm and 5 mm and can be found either on both surfaces (interior and exterior) or on the interior surface only. The original surface and the core are red (Munsell 2.5YR, 6/8) to pink (Munsell 7.5YR, 8/3). Walls' thickness is c.I mm to 1 cm. Decoration mcludes incisions, slip and glaze, forming geometric and floral motifs

This is well-known and described coarse hand-made earthenware with smooth fracture and medium firing. It is always unglazed and manufactured for cooking vessels. The surface is black, grey and red with brown and grey rounded and angular inclusions of c.1 mm to 1 cm in size and covers 20/0 to 6% of the sherd. Walls' thickness is c. 1-8 mm. Decoration often include incised horizontal and wavy lines and projected bands with and without incised decoration.
parallels: Kush and al-Mataf (Kennet 2004: 53-56; figs.19-25, pp: 116-122)

description: Thin, light coarse ware with combed or rope prints decoration that cover the whole body. The fabric has smooth fracture and medium firing. The surface colours are either white or gray. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 8 cm.
parallels: Sohar excavation (Kervran 2004: fig. 9, Nos. 7-8, pp: 274 & fig. 10, Nos.1 O- 11, pp: 277) & Surface pottery from Kashm Nadir in northern Oman (de Cardi & Doe 1971: figs. 15, No. 77: & 50, No.1)

description: This class of porcelain is well-known. Kennet (2004: 51) describes it as ..... a well-established class of porcelain. It is covered with a transparent lime-alkali glaze and decorated with underglaze cobalt. It is possible to sub-divide and date CBW based on the style of decoration ... ". The ware includes small white and brown grits. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 1 cm.
parallels: Kush and al-Mataf (Kennet 2004: 51-52) & Surface collection from Northern Oman (de Cardi & Doe 1971: figs. 12, Nos.1-16, pp: 261; figs. 45-47)

description: Medium coarse, well-fired glazed earthenware with conchoidal fracture. The original surface and core are pale yellow to a light grey and a pinky red. The glaze is often on both the interior and exterior surfaces and sometimes on the interior only. This glaze varies in colour between green, light-green, olive, brown, greenish-brown and black. It could also be blue (see below BBAHW). Walls' thickness varies from 1 mm to 2 cm.
parallels: Whitcomb 1975: 129; al-Mataf: KHUNJ (Kennet 2004: 42-43)

description: This class is similar to the Bahia Ware (BAHLA) but it is distinguished by its blue glaze that covers both the interior and the exterior surfaces. It is coarse ware with smooth fracture and medium firing. The fabric original surface is light red (Munsell ~.5YR, 7/6), and the core is light red (Munsell 2.5YR, 7/6). Walls' thickness is c.3 mm to 1 cm. This class has only found on one site (CS.4.1.1) in al-Qaryatain village. and it has not been recorded at any other site, at least within the surveyed area. The sherds might belong to one single large open-mouthed jar.
parallels: No parallels with the blue colour but the fabric is similar to the above indicated Bahia ware with parallels from al-Mataf (Kennet 2004: 42-43)

description: This ware is well-defined through the literature as "Longquan Celadon". Kennet (2004: 49) states that "Longquan Celadon has a good quality, light grey stoneware body covered in a thick green glaze that often crazes very finely. There is some variation in the fabric and glaze colour. This is a well-established class of ceramic that was widely exported to the Western Indian Ocean, the Near East, and the Mediterranean ... ". Walls' thickness is c 1-8 mm.
parallels: Kush (Kennet 2004: fig.I8, pp: 115)

description: Chinese porcelain fired under certain low degrees of temperatures and includes a very fine red-coloured enamelled decoration over thick or transparent glazes (Kennet 2004: 52). Walls' thickness is 1 mm to 1 cm.
parallels: AI-Mataf (Kennet: 2004: 52).

description: This class includes all the coarse wares that bear similar characteristics of both Middle Islamic and Late Islamic periods in which it was difficult to assign them with certainty to any of these two periods. Thus, they were classified under the broader chronological period between both Middle Islamic and Late Islamic.

This class describes a recent type of porcelain that had been manufactured in Europe. The tradition might be developed from the Far Eastern porcelain. This class was largely produced and exported to widespread area, including the Oman Peninsula in which it had been used until the late 1970s. It is decorated with painted and glazed geometric and floral motifs. The ware itself is thin and medium fired with smooth fracture. Walls' thickness is c.1-8 mm.
parallels: Kush and al-Mataf (Kennet 2004: 44).

description: This class belongs to the most recent Far Eastern porcelain types, and it is often decorated with transfers (Kennet 2004: 52). It is largely produced and imported to widespread area, including the Oman Peninsula in which has been used for long time even until recent times. Walls' thickness is c.1 mm to 1 cm.
origin: Far East origin (mainly Japan in the 20 th century)
parallels: Al-Mataf (Kennet 2004: 52-53)

description: Thin, medium-fired coarse unglazed earthenware with smooth fracture. The surface is light yellow to creamy white and the core is light gray to yellow with white, brown and gray small rounded inclusions of c.1-2 mm in size and covers 1% of the body sherd. Decoration includes incised wavy and horizontal lines. Walls' thickness is c.1-6 mm.
parallels: Kush and al-Mataf(Kennet 2004: 57, figs. 28, types 109-110: pp: 124).

description: This class includes all the coarse wares that bear similar characteristics of both Late Islamic and Recent periods in which it was difficult to assign them with certainty to any of these two periods. Thus, they were classified under the broader chronological period between both Late Islamic and Recent.
This class includes all the unidentifiable earthenware sherds that bear glaze or traces of glaze. These are small and/or badly eroded. Walls' thickness varies between 1 mm and 1 cm.
